title: Robot - How Do I?
description:
Robot - How Do I?

Robot - How Do I...?

How do I create robot points?

How do I manage robot point files?

How do I use robot tools?

How do I use robot locals?

How can I move the robot from one point to another in a straight line?

How can I move the robot through points without stopping?

How do I achieve optimum robot speed for my application?

How do I move a robot from one controller to another?

How do I create robot points?

Teach points from the Robot Manager Jog & Teach page.  Open the Robot Manager from the Tools menu and select the Jog & Teach page.
  Select a point and click the Teach button.

				Teach points from Robot Manager Points page.  Open the Robot Manager from the Tools menu and select the Points page.  Type in new point data.

				Enter point data in a point editor.  Open a point file by selecting Open from File menu.

				Define points in the Command Window.  Open the Command Windows from the Tools menu.  Enter point assignment commands.  Then execute SavePoints to save the point data.

How do I manage robot point files?

There is one fixed point file in every project: Points.pts. You can add more point files to your project.

To add a point file:

			Select New from the File menu.
			Select the Points file type.
			Enter a name for the new point file.
			Click OK.

In your programs, you can use SavePoints to save a point file in the controller flash memory.  If you save a point file that is not in the current project, it will reside only in the controller flash memory.

How do I use robot tools?

Robot tools allow you to define the location of an end effector that is mounted to the last joint of your robot.  For SCARA robots, this is joint 4 and for six axis robots, this is joint 6.
  You can define up to three tools.  Tool 0 is always defined and cannot be changed.  By default, the tool number is 0.

The tool coordinate system

See Tool Coordinate Systems

Advantages of using tools

			Robot points do not have to be re-taught after an end effector has been changed.  Only the tool needs to be redefined.
			It is easier to teach points that require rotation of the end effector.

How to define tools

There are two ways to define tools:

			Use the Tool page on the Robot Manager to manually enter coordinates.  There is also a Tool Wizard that automatically calculates tool offsets from two points.
			Use the TLSet statement in your program or from the command window.

Tool statements and functions

Tool Statement	Select a tool that has been defined.

Tool Function	Returns the current tool number.

TLSet Statement	Defines a tool.

TLDef Function	Returns whether a tool has been defined.

TLClr Statement	Clears (undefines) a tool.

How to use tools in a program

Once one or more tools have been defined, you need to select them in your program.  To select the current tool definition, use the Tool statement.

The following example uses a robot with an end effector that has two vacuum grippers.  A tool is defined for each gripper.

Function PickPlace

		  Tool 1

		  Jump Pick1

		  On Vac1

		  Wait .1

		  Tool 2

		  Jump Pick2

		  On Vac2

		  Wait .1

		  Tool 1

		  Jump Place1

		  Off Vac1

		  Wait .1

		  Tool 2

		  Jump Place2

		  Off Vac2

		  Wait .1

		Fend

How do I use robot locals?

Robot locals allow you to define local coordinate systems within the robot base coordinate system.

The local coordinate system

See Local Coordinate Systems

Advantages of using locals

			Robot points do not have to be re-taught after the location of a fixture in the work cell has been changed.  Only the local needs to be redefined.
			Locals can be defined in 3D.  This allows the robot to move to positions in a plane that is not parallel with the base.
			It is easier to jog and teach points in a local when it is rotated or skewed.

How to define locals

There are two ways to define locals:

			Use the Local page on the Robot Manager to manually enter coordinates.  There is also a Local Wizard that automatically walks you through created a local.
			Use the Local statement in your program or from the command window.

Local statements and functions

Local Statement	Defines a local coordinate system.

LocalDef Function	Returns whether a local has been defined.

LocalClr Statement	Clears (undefines) a local.

How can I move the robot from one point to another in a straight line?

Use the Move command to move the robot in a straight line from the current position to a target position.

How can I move the robot through points without stopping?

Use CP (continuous path) motion.

How do I achieve robot optimum speed for my application?

Weight.
  Ensure that you have correctly set the Weight parameters for the payload you are using.  Open the Robot Manager from the Tools menu and select the Weight page.

				Inertia.
  Ensure that you have set the Inertia parameters. Open the Robot Manager from the Tools menu and select the Inertia page.

				Arch.
  Use Arch for your Jump statements to optimize jump motion.   Open the Robot Manager from the Tools menu and select the Arch page.

				CP.
  In some cases, you may want to use continuous path motion, especially when the robot needs to move through a point to clear an obstacle.   CP motion enables the robot to move near a point with minimal deceleration.
  Also see CP Motion Speed / Acceleration and Tool Orienation.

				Fine.
  In some cases, you may want to increase the Fine settings.  This can allow the robot to achieve final position more quickly, but with less repeatability.  For more information on Fine, see Controlling position accuracy.

How do I move a robot from one controller to another?

Follow these steps to move a robot from one controller to another:

			First, ensure that the destination controller has the correct motor amplifiers.  Select Controller from the Setup menu, then select the Robot Amplifiers page to view the amplifier power values.
			On the source controller, save the calibration data.  Select Controller from the Setup menu, then select the Robot Calibration page.
  Click the Save Cal button to save the calibration data to a file.
			Change the robot model on the destination controller.  Select Controller from the Setup menu, then select the Robot Model page.  Select the model of the robot you will be connecting.
			Load the robot calibration data.  Select Controller from the Setup menu, then select the Robot Calibration page.
  Click the Load Cal button to load the calibration data from the file that was created in step 2 above.
			Connect the robot to the destination controller.
			Verify operation of the robot.
examples:
Function PickPlace
		  Tool 1
		  Jump Pick1
		  On Vac1
		  Wait .1
		  Tool 2
		  Jump Pick2
		  On Vac2
		  Wait .1
		  Tool 1
		  Jump Place1
		  Off Vac1
		  Wait .1
		  Tool 2
		  Jump Place2
		  Off Vac2
		  Wait .1
		Fend
